My main research focuses on the evolution of the stellar populations in open and globular clusters located in our Galaxy. 
These clusters provide the laboratories necessary to constrain our current models of stellar formation, chemical evolution, and cluster dynamics.

### Globular Clusters

#### -NGC 5024 and NGC 5053

My thesis works focuses on a pair of Globular clusters, M53 (NGC 5024) and NGC 5053, which have recently been found to have large tidal tails, and possibly, a bridge like structure between the clusters. These morphological features suggest that these clusters have not only experienced strong interactions with the Galaxy, but also possibly with each other. For this research, I am collecting new, high quality photometric and spectroscopic data, to pair with N-body simulations in order create a more complete picture of how these features, and the clusters themselves, formed and are evolving. This work will provide a more dynamically accurate description of cluster formation and evolution in the Milky Way.

#### ---> Observations

I have collected the data for my thesis project using the Wisconsin-Indiana-Yale-NOAO (WIYN) 3.5-meter and 0.9-meter telescopes at the
Kitt Peak National Observatory near Tucson Arizona. On the WIYN 3.5-meter telescope, I have used the partial One Degree Imager (pODI)
to collect wide field photometric data around the two clusters listed above. I have also used the Hydra multi-object spectrograph
on the 3.5 meter telescope to collect high resolution spectra of cluster members. I have used the MOSIAC1.1 imager and the Half Degree
Imager (HDI) on the 0.9 meter telescope to collect additional photometry of the clusters.


#### ---> Simulations

In the future I will be using my observational data to help constrain N-Body simulations of these clusters to better understand their
formation and evolution as they orbit the Galaxy. These simulations will be done with the NBODY6 code running on Indiana University's
Big Red II supercomputer. For these simulations I will be working with Dr. Enrico Vesperini, who is also at IU.

### Open Clusters

#### -NGC 6791
NGC 6791 is a very peculiar open cluster in the Milky May. The mass and age of this cluster are characteristic of an object somewhere between an open cluster and a globular cluster. Due to these physical characteristics, studies have attempted to determine if NGC 6791 shows the same chemical signatures found in globular clusters. My research on this cluster has used spectra available from the Sloan Extension for Galactic Understanding and Exploration (SEGUE) survey to search for CN and CH abundance variations among cluster members, indicative of multiple populations.


### Instrumentation & Software

#### -APO 1-meter Telescope
Working with Dr. Thomas Harrison at my undergraduate institution, New Mexico State University (NMSU), I wrote the software to control a high speed 5-band photometer on the 1-meter NMSU telescope at the Apache Point Observatory. The software was written in LabView and provided an easy to use graphical user interface that controlled the data acquisition and telescope positioning.

#### -2.5-meter Sloan Telescope
During the summer before my graduate courses started, I undertook and additional software design project at NMSU with Dr. Jon Holtzman.  My role was to create a program that would allow observers to use the Apache Point Observatory Galactic Evolution Experiment (APOGEE) spectrograph, located on the 2.5 meter Sloan telescope, from the adjacent NMSU 1-meter telescope.  This allows observers to collect spectra of astronomical objects during bright time (near full moon) on a smaller aperture telescope, when the larger aperture Sloan telescope cannot be used. The software package I wrote for this project was written in Python.

### Big Data and Astronomy
While taking classes at IU, I have been introduced to the techniques used in data mining and machine learning through
graduate courses in the Computer Science department. Specifically, I took CS-565 (Data Mining) with Dr. Mehmet Dalkilic. Throughout
this class I became very interested in applying data mining and machine learning techniques to astronomical data. As we continue
to make advancements in astronomy, we will undertake bigger and bigger surveys, such as LSST and GAIA. The amount of data that these
surveys, and others, will produce is going to require new sophisticated algorithms to efficiently make sense of it all. 

#### ---> ParaHeap-k (An improved parallel clustering algorithm)
For a final project in my data mining class, I worked with other graduate students, and Dr. Dalkilic, to develop a new and improved version of the
k-means algorithm to classify stars in a simulated Milky Way catalog into one of the three Galactic components: thin disk, thick disk,and halo. The work
that we did was published in a special issue of the [September 2014 IEEE Computer Society journal Computer: Computing in Astronomy](http://ieeexplore.ieee.org/Xplore/defdeny.jsp?url=http%3A%2F%2Fieeexplore.ieee.org%2Fstamp%2Fstamp.jsp%3Ftp%3D%26arnumber%3D6898791&denyReason=-134&arnumber=6898791&productsMatched=null&userType=inst).
